We have a member, Steven Oliver, works with Marshall Arts School, gets them to do a very good job with this, rent the whole movie theater. Right. He's in a small talk of the community and that's what he does to fill this fraction. That's it. That's what he does. He says, I can't handle all the business as it is. So again, not a strategy, a referral strategy that a lot of people are using, but think about again, the question is how could you use that? How can you use it? Then start it. You don't have to start with 4,000 people. Like you talked about the cardio start with 100 people. 100 people, the next thing grows to 1,000. So that's how you do it. Now, one of the things, again, this is a little bit of advanced stuff that we're talking about here in the final session, but you talk about how that all referrals don't come just from clients. When people think of referrals, they think of coming from clients, customers, or patients. You talk about phishing upstream to get referrals. What does that mean? Well, so somebody has your customer or has your client or has your patient now has a customer in a non-competitive environment. They have a whole bunch of them because customers of a feather flock together. So the first thing to think about is how can I somehow make you want to tell your customers about me? And you don't have to be my customer necessarily for that to happen. So most professions have peer-to-peer referrals. They're sort of familiar with that. But so lawyers will get referrals from other referrals or from other attorneys. And the guy who specializes in DUI will get a referral from a business attorney who doesn't handle DUI, but his client has now gotten picked up, driving drunk. So everybody's familiar with that, but that's pretty narrow thinking. And so you can be much broader in cultivating these relationships. Ben Glass, who runs a personal injury law practice, remember ours is very smart about it for his practice. So he does a couple of things. One, he actually runs a local mixed breed of dog business mastermind group, really his own networking group, where he brings other kinds of business people together. But typically ones who have families as customers, what he is calling, because somebody's going to have an accident and they're going to tell somebody about it. He does his online TV show and has you on as a guest and gives you a copy of it that you can go use yourself and promotes you, you, the local high-end furniture store, who you notify all your customers, I'm going to be on this thing. So a lot of this can get forced by sort of paying it forward, helping other people who then in turn let you into their list. A lot of times you can do it with content if you are prolific with content. And you, I'm in the content business, but you don't have to be in the content business. Obviously Ben can provide all sorts of content about what you need to know if you're in an accident, what you need to know to hire an attorney, and what you need to know to fire an attorney. And financial planners can use that in their newsletters, they can use that on their websites. Insurance people can use that in their newsletters and use it on their website. So you can be a content pusher.
